title: Pyrography decoration on a spoon handle
subtitle: Burning a simple repeating pattern onto a carved spoon handle
excerpt: Decorating a carved and finished spoon handle with pyrography: transferring a repeating border design, burning the outlines with a writing tip, and filling the shaded areas with a shading tip. A good first pyrography project on a piece you have already made.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for pyrography technique and domestic decoration. Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on poker-work decoration of household wooden objects.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of burning designs into wood using a heated metal tip. Also called poker work or wood burning.
A small pointed tip for line work, outlines, and lettering in pyrography.
A flat or spoon-shaped tip that contacts the wood over a wider area, used for filling and smooth tonal areas in pyrography.

content.text: The burning tip reaches temperatures that will cause an instant burn on skin contact. Never touch the tip directly. Rest the unit on the cooling stand between strokes. Allow the tip to cool fully before changing attachments.
A spoon handle is one of the easiest surfaces to decorate with

: it is small, it sits steadily in the hand, and a simple repeating border pattern suits the long narrow shape. The spoon must be fully carved, sanded, and completely dry before burning; any residual moisture causes uneven burn depth.

Sand the handle to 400 grit and wipe off all dust. Do not apply any oil or finish before burning; oil prevents even penetration of the burn and causes the tip to smoke and spit. Burning is always done on a bare, dry wood surface. Finish with oil after the pyrography is complete.

A simple repeating border of diagonal hatching, chevrons, or interlocking diamonds works well on a spoon handle. Draw the pattern on paper at the correct size. Transfer it to the handle using graphite transfer paper: tape the paper design over the handle, insert a strip of transfer paper between them, and trace each line firmly with a ballpoint pen. Check the transfer is complete before removing the tape.

and allow the burner to reach operating temperature. Test on a scrap of the same species first. Burn all the outline lines, pulling the tip toward you at a consistent speed. Keep the handle resting on the work surface; holding it in the air introduces wobble.

after the outlines are complete. Use the tip to fill alternate diamonds or chevrons with an even tone. Sweep the tip in one direction across each area, keeping the strokes parallel and close together. Let the tool cool on the stand between areas.

Wipe off any carbon residue with a dry cloth. Apply a single coat of raw linseed oil or food-safe board butter to the handle. The oil darkens the unburned wood slightly and makes the burned areas stand out more clearly by contrast.
Name or date on the reverse: burn text on the back of the handle as a maker's mark. Use the writing tip and transfer paper as above.
Botanical motif: a single leaf or flower repeat suits a longer handle (18 cm or more). Transfer the motif to the widest part of the handle.

rows.symptom: Burn depth is uneven across the same line, lighter in some places and darker in others
rows.cause: Varying pull speed along the stroke
rows.fix: Work shorter strokes of 30 to 40 mm rather than trying to pull the full length in one pass. Consistent speed over a shorter distance is easier to maintain.

rows.symptom: Shading tip leaves visible directional streaks in the filled area
rows.cause: Sweeping strokes not close enough together
rows.fix: Overlap each stroke by half its width. The strokes should blend into each other rather than show as distinct lines.
